title-group.article-title: Optimized Distance Holders Improve Precision in Fractional Laser Treatment

aff.named-content: Department of Dermatology, Harvard Medical School, Massachusetts General Hospital

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Lasers in Surgery and Medicine published by Wiley Periodicals LLC.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Fractional Photothermolysis (FP) relies on precise laser focus to create microscopic treatment zones (MTZs). Focal plane deviations, caused by handpiece pressure and movement, shift the treated tissue out of focus, increasing spot size and reducing lesion depth, compromising treatment efficacy. This study investigates an optimized spacer to mitigate load‐induced deviations and improve clinical outcomes.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A difference frequency generation (DFG) laser system (IPG Medical) with 3050/3200 nm wavelength treated ex vivo human abdominoplasty skin, mounted on a load‐adjustable platform (0–1000 g, ±0.1 g). Tissue was treated at 5 mJ/pulse with a nominal spot size of 40 µm using spacers ranging from 2 × 2 mm2 to 8 × 8 mm2 and an optimized 2 × 2 mm2 grid spacer. Optical coherence tomography measured skin bulging and focal plane deviations, while histological analysis validated lesion depth and diameter. Knife‐edge technique projections were used to assess theoretical beam behavior.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Applied load caused skin bulging, resulting in a linear increase in lesion diameter and a linear decrease in depth. Smaller spacers (2 × 2 mm2) reduced focal deviations and maintained MTZ precision within the Rayleigh range. 2 × 2 mm2 grid spacers further reduced bulging by redistributing the load and ensuring more uniform MTZ formation.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Load‐induced focal shifts significantly impact FP efficiency, particularly on nonuniform skin surfaces. An optimized spacer enhanced precision by minimizing bulging, allowing treatments to remain within one Rayleigh length. These findings highlight the clinical importance of spacer selection in achieving consistent FP outcomes, particularly for small‐spot laser applications. Clinicians should integrate smaller or grid‐patterned spacers to enhance procedural accuracy.

institution-wrap.institution: The authors received no specific funding for this work.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: For two decades, fractional photothermolysis (FP) has been utilized in facial treatments, while also demonstrating efficacy in addressing scars and birthmarks [1]. FP is recognized as a highly effective modality due to its role in generating microscopic treatment zones (MTZs) at precise depths without injuring surrounding tissue [2]. It has primarily been used for facial treatments due to the higher density of pilosebaceous units in the skin, which promotes faster wound healing and a lower risk of scarring compared to non‐facial skin [3]. Consequently, the face typically re‐epithelializes more rapidly than other body areas with lower hair density [4].

Recently, there has been a growing interest in expanding fractional lasers beyond facial treatments to other body parts for applications including skin rejuvenation, wrinkle removal, skin tightening, and metabolic stimulation [5, 6]. Additionally, laser‐assisted drug delivery (LADD) depends on precise and reproducible fractional ablation to achieve controlled transdermal and intradermal delivery. Consistent ablation patterns are critical for reliable dosimetry, as parameters such as channel depth, density, and thermal coagulation significantly affect drug diffusion and bioavailability [7, 8]. In non‐facial areas, one of the most critical factors is focal spot size, as it is well established that smaller spot sizes (< 100 µm diameter) promote faster wound healing and precise MTZ generation. In contrast, facial treatments use spot sizes < 500 µm with some studies suggesting an optimal size around 150 µm [1, 9, 10, 11]. However, achieving even smaller focal spots presents challenges, including a reduced Rayleigh length, increasing the risk of defocusing, and reducing treatment precision. A defocused beam alters spot size, generating larger MTZs and increasing unwanted tissue damage [12]. To achieve optimal results, maintaining laser focus typically within one Rayleigh length of the beam waist is critical for optimal FP treatment.

Current focal distance spacers are often square or ring‐shaped, with openings greater than 1 cm2, making it challenging to maintain the focus [13]. Larger spacers can increase skin bulging, particularly when excessive pressure is applied by clinicians or when treating curved anatomical regions. This study aims to optimize spacer design for small focal spot sizes (< 100 µm) by minimizing focal deviations and maintaining laser precision. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) was employed to quantify the skin bulging with various spacer designs and sizes [14, 15]. This approach enables the development of spacers that enhance precision and outcomes for non‐facial fractional laser treatments. Based on our results and initial data collected, an optimized spacer design was chosen to potentially increase the efficacy of treatment with small, focused spot sizes.

sec.title: Ex Vivo Skin Load Experiments
sec.p: Abdominoplasty skin samples from 4 patients were used. 3D printed focal distance spacers were placed on the tissue samples and analyzed via OCT imaging (Figure 1). The tissue and spacer were positioned on a precision scale, enabling controlled load application via the OCT probe head, ranging from 50 to 1200 g with an accuracy of ±0.1 g. OCT imaging was employed to quantitatively assess tissue bulging as a function of applied load and spacer dimensions. Subsequently, the skin was laser‐treated under controlled conditions, with different spacer sizes and different loads, and OCT results were correlated with histological results to validate our findings.

caption.p: Depicting the OCT imaging setup shown with (A) low and (B) high load, respectively, showing increased skin bulging. (C) Depicts a laser treatment where the handpiece is pressed onto the skin with high load, resulting in skin bulging and focal displacement.

sec.title: Spacer Design and Printing
sec.p: Focal distance spacers for the laser handpiece were designed in SolidWorks (Concord, MA) as single‐part components with varying aperture sizes, including square openings of 0.5 × 0.5 mm², 1 × 1 mm², 2 × 2 mm², 3 × 3 mm², 4 × 4 mm², 5 × 5 mm², 6 × 6 mm², 7 × 7 mm², and 8 × 8 mm², as well as a 2 × 2 mm² grid‐patterned spacer (Figure 2). All spacers were 3D printed using PA11 Black material (PA850, Protolabs, Maple Plain, MN).

caption.p: OCT images of skin bulging based on load using (A) 2 × 2 mm2, (B) 4 × 4 mm2, (C) 8 × 8 mm2, and (D) 2 × 2 mm2 grid spacers. Vertical comparisons depict increasing loads of 0, 400, and 1000 g, while horizontal comparisons show variations in spacer size. Skin bulging was shown to be greater with increasing load and also shown to be greater with increasing spacer size. The 2 × 2 mm2 grid spacer exhibited the least bulging.

sec.title: Optical Coherence Imaging
sec.p: An OCT scanner (TEL220C1, Thorlabs) with a central wavelength of 1300 nm was used to acquire B‐Scan images with an axial resolution of 4.2 µm and a lateral resolution of 13 µm (LSM03, Thorlabs). OCT imaging quantified skin bulging under varying loads across different spacer sizes (Figure 2).

sec.title: Rayleigh Range
sec.p: While smaller laser spot sizes may be advantageous for certain applications, they also shorten the Rayleigh length, causing the beam to defocus more rapidly. This defocusing can compromise treatment accuracy by generating broader, less confined MTZs and reducing precision in energy delivery. For optimal treatment precision, it is critical to position the target tissue within one Rayleigh length of the laser's focal plane. The Rayleigh length, Zr, is the distance from the beam waist, where the beam is narrowest, to the point where its width increases by a factor of √2, doubling the cross‐sectional area [13]. Zr=πωo2λ=12kωo2 λ is wavelength, ωo is beam waist, and k is wave number k = 2πλ.

Table 1 presents three dermatological treatment lasers, each characterized by distinct wavelengths. It specifically highlights the Rayleigh range associated with commonly utilized spot sizes ranging from 40 to 170 µm in diameter. Notably, at a spot size of 40 µm, the Rayleigh range falls below 0.5 mm, indicating that even minimal deviations from the focal plane can result in a significant reduction in treatment accuracy.

caption.p: Rayleigh range of three dermatological treatment lasers with varying wavelengths as a function of focused spot size. As spot size decreases, the Rayleigh range decreases significantly, making precise focal alignment essential for maintaining treatment accuracy.

sec.title: Laser Parameters
sec.p: Laser ablation was performed with a difference frequency generation (DFG) prototype system (IPG Medical Corporation), which has previously been described in detail [9, 13]. The setup consisted of the DFG handpiece mounted to a stand and skin tissue placed on a laboratory jack and scale system, which allowed for load adjustments with an accuracy of ±0.1 g. For the spacer sizes 2 × 2 mm2, 4 × 4 mm2, and 8 × 8 mm2 loads of 0, 50, 100, 400, and 1000 g were applied to each piece of tissue from 4 subjects, and a fractional scanning pattern with 5 mJ energy per pulse was applied. The loads and spacer sizes were chosen to span a wide range of focal displacements, from 0 mm to approximately 1.9 mm, in multiple‐step increments. The exposures were performed on large pieces of tissue with trimmed fat to create a flat skin surface. The resulting samples were histologically analyzed posttreatment to correlate focal deviation with lesion depth and diameter.

sec.title: Knife‐Edge Technique
sec.p: Knife‐edge technique is a method involving measuring the radius of a Gaussian beam by gradually blocking a laser beam with a sharp edge while measuring the corresponding transmitted power. This provides information about the beam intensity profile across a cross‐section, with the highest intensity at the center and an exponential decrease from the center. Essentially, a sharp edge is moved perpendicularly to the propagation of a laser, and the total transmitted power is measured as a function of the edge position [16].

sec.title: Histology
sec.p: Tissue samples were obtained from the center of each treatment area, embedded in optimal cutting temperature compound (Tissue‐Tek O.C.T. Compound, Sakura Finetek USA Inc., Torrance, CA), and stored at −20°C until processing. Tissue samples were then mounted in a cryostat and serially cut in 20 µm increments vertically and horizontally. Frozen sections were placed on microscope slides and stained with nitro‐blue tetrazolium chloride (NBTC) (N5514, N8129, P5493, Sigma–Aldrich, Saint Louis, MO) in a dark incubator for 3 h before being dehydrated, fixed, and cover‐slipped. The NBTC staining method was employed to differentiate viable from thermally coagulated tissue, enabling precise histological assessment of laser‐induced injury. NBTC stains viable tissue blue, whereas thermally damaged regions remain unstained, providing a clear contrast for delineation of the coagulation zone. The depth and lateral extent of tissue injury were assessed using high‐resolution brightfield imaging, acquired via a digital slide scanner (NanoZoomer S60; Hamamatsu). Quantitative measurements of the thermal coagulation zone were performed at a consistent tissue depth of ~60 µm across all laser settings to enable comparative analysis.

sec.title: Relationship of Spacer Size and Load
sec.p: As shown in Figure 3, larger spacers resulted in greater focal displacement for the same applied load compared to smaller spacers. In all cases, the focal displacement increases in a logarithmic fashion to the applied load, suggesting a diminishing displacement rate beyond certain load thresholds. Differences in focal displacement between spacer sizes became more pronounced at higher loads, indicating that the influence of spacer size on focal displacement becomes increasingly significant as the applied load increases. Finally, the focal displacement increases more steeply for larger spacers at lower loads, implying that they are more responsive initially to changes in load. In this study, load was used instead of pressure as the relevant metric because pressure, defined as force per unit area, is primarily determined by the contact surface area, which is set by the spacer size rather than the applied load.

caption.p: OCT image analysis of skin bulging in response to increasing spacer loads demonstrated a logarithmic relationship between load application and focal displacement across all spacer sizes. The most pronounced focal displacement occurred within the initial 200 g loading range. Additionally, a reduction in spacer size corresponded to a decrease in focal displacement, as evidenced by the visibly lower displacement of the red line (0.5 × 0.5 mm²) compared to the purple line (8 × 8 mm²). Notably, the grid spacer demonstrated superior accuracy in reducing skin bulging compared to the single‐opening spacer of equivalent size.

sec.title: Histological Analysis
sec.p: As illustrated in Figure 4, increasing focal displacement due to skin bulging results in a reduction in lesion depth and an expansion in lesion diameter. This is attributed to a decrease in energy density caused by the beam being out of the focal plane, which results in an increase in beam waist. Specifically, the ablation hole diameter expands from 55 µm at the focal point to 153 µm when displaced by just 1.8 mm, which is a deviation that commonly occurs in clinical practice. Correspondingly, the ablation depth decreases from the desired 760 µm to only 45 µm, which could substantially reduce treatment efficacy. Consistent with prior reports, the thermal damage zone remains relatively constant despite focal displacement [11]. We observe a coagulation thickness of approximately 20 µm at a tissue depth of 60 µm across all applied settings, including out‐of‐focus lesions. This indicates that thermal diffusion plays the dominant role in shaping coagulation zone morphology rather than focal spot size or displacement from the focal plane. For this laser system, the Rayleigh range is approximately 0.4 mm. When skin bulging remains within this range, the ablation zone depth and diameter exhibit minimal deviation from those observed when the laser is precisely aligned with the focal plane.

caption.p: Histological analysis of focal displacement. The depth and diameter of MTZs are significantly influenced by focal displacement due to skin bulging. When the skin remains in focus (0 mm displacement), the MTZ depth reaches 760 µm with a diameter of 55 µm. However, at a focal displacement of 1.8 mm, the MTZ depth is markedly reduced to 45 µm, while the diameter increases to 153 µm. This shift results in shallower and broader MTZs, highlighting the critical impact of focal accuracy on treatment precision.

sec.p: In Figure 5A, the histologically measured ablation zone diameter is compared to the laser beam diameter determined by using the knife‐edge method [14]. The data demonstrates that the laser beam expands exponentially with increasing deviation from the focal plane. As anticipated, greater displacement of the skin from the laser's focal plane results in a broader ablation zone, indicating reduced precision. Interestingly, the MTZ diameter increases nearly linearly, rather than following the exponential divergence of the laser beam, suggesting that the interaction between the expanding beam and tissue is influenced by additional factors beyond geometric optics alone.

caption.p: Correlation between histological analysis and laser beam characteristics. The relationship between ablation zone diameter and laser beam size as a function of focal plane deviation demonstrates a linear increase in histologically measured lesion diameter, whereas the measured beam diameter follows an exponential increase. Similarly, histological analysis of ablation zone depth reveals a relatively linear decrease, in contrast to the exponential decay observed in radiant exposure, as determined by the knife‐edge technique. These findings suggest that tissue response to laser energy deviates from theoretical beam propagation models, potentially due to nonlinear thermal diffusion and tissue‐specific optical properties.

sec.p: Similarly, in Figure 5B, the ablation zone depth is compared to the radiant exposure at varying distances from the focal plane, calculated based on pulse energy and beam waist size. Histological analysis reveals a linear decrease in ablation depth, whereas the radiant exposure exhibits an exponential decline with increasing focal displacement. While less pronounced than anticipated, the histological results underscore the critical importance of precise focal positioning, as minor deviations from the focal plane result in a substantial reduction in ablation depth. These findings highlight the necessity of maintaining focal accuracy to achieve the intended depth of tissue ablation and ensure treatment precision.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The results demonstrate that smaller spacer openings significantly reduce skin bulging, thereby enhancing treatment precision. However, using a laser handpiece with a small aperture poses practical challenges, as it requires frequent repositioning to ensure full treatment coverage, particularly over larger anatomical areas such as the body. To address this limitation, we developed a grid‐lock pattern spacer, which integrates small apertures to minimize skin bulging while simultaneously allowing for efficient treatment of larger areas without excessive repositioning. This design balances precision and procedural efficiency, making it more feasible for clinical applications.

Experimental findings indicate that skin bulging exhibits a nonlinear response to applied load, with the most significant increase occurring at lower loads. Beyond a certain threshold load, further increases result in only minimal additional bulging, suggesting a logarithmic relationship between focal displacement and applied load. This behavior could be strategically used by positioning the laser's focal plane within the anticipated bulging region, ensuring that, under a specific range of applied loads, tissue remains within one Rayleigh length of the focal point. Such an approach could enhance treatment precision by reducing excessive focal deviation, thereby improving procedural consistency and accuracy.

To provide context for the applied loading conditions in this study, the weight of several ablative and non‐ablative fractional laser handpieces such as the DFG prototype used in this study, as well as commercial handpieces like the DeepFX (Lumenis), ProFractional (Sciton), and Fraxel Re:store (Reliant), have been analyzed. The weight of these handpieces ranged from ~300 to ~800 g. While articulated‐arm systems often incorporate counterweights to minimize the effective load on the skin, fiber‐based laser systems frequently lack such mechanical compensation. Given that our results show significant skin deformation with as little as 200 g of applied load, the resting weight of these handpieces alone, even without any active downward force by the operator, is sufficient to cause significant tissue bulging.

The comparison between a single‐opening spacer and a grid‐patterned spacer with multiple openings of the same size demonstrated superior performance of the grid design in minimizing skin bulging. This effect is likely due to the localized bulging concentration observed with single‐opening spacers, whereas the grid spacer design distributes skin displacement across multiple openings, allowing excess tissue to expand through several outlets rather than accumulating in one area. By reducing localized bulging, the grid spacer helps maintain tissue positioning within the Rayleigh range of the laser beam, thereby improving FP efficiency and focal plane stability.

It was interesting to note that measured laser characteristics and histological results did not align, which could be attributed to self‐focusing and other nonlinear thermal effects [17]. Additional factors include changing tissue properties from freeze‐thaw or thermal stress such as laser–tissue interactions, thermal conduction, shrinkage, or tissue dehydration could also influence final ablation crater sizes [18]. Apart from an improved spacer design, one could design fractional lasers with extended focus as a Bessel beam rather than a Gaussian beam, which is the current standard. Bessel beams allow for deeper, narrower MTZs, which would help with extending the focal plane and delivering equal intensity laser pulses [19].

One limitation of the grid spacer design is the potential partial obstruction of laser beams due to the mesh structure, which may result in untreated skin regions. To determine whether this leads to undesirable treatment patterns or clinical outcomes, further in vivo studies are necessary. However, improvements of treatment zones may compensate for these gaps, highlighting the need for further investigation to refine the grid pattern and maximize overall treatment accuracy. Future iterations of the grid spacer should focus on optimizing the structural design to minimize material usage while maximizing treatment area coverage, an aspect not addressed in this study.

Another limitation of this study is that all experiments were conducted on ex vivo tissue, which does not fully replicate clinical conditions, particularly in terms of skin hydration and biomechanical properties. Prior research has demonstrated that skin hydration significantly influences tissue response and bulging dynamics [20], suggesting that further in vivo validation is required.

Additionally, while reducing laser spot size to sub‐40 µm levels may theoretically enhance wound healing, our findings indicate that such reductions may be impractical due to the increased sensitivity to minor focal deviations, which could compromise treatment accuracy. These factors must be carefully considered when designing future FP laser systems to balance precision with clinical feasibility.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study demonstrates that load‐induced deviations significantly impact the focal plane in FP, reducing treatment efficiency by increasing lesion diameter and decreasing lesion depth. By systematically evaluating spacer designs, we found that smaller spacers (2 × 2 mm2) effectively minimized bulging, maintaining MTZ precision within the Rayleigh range. Furthermore, the 2 × 2 mm2 grid spacer provided additional stability, redistributing pressure to further reduce focal displacement and improve treatment uniformity.

These findings have important clinical implications, particularly for FP treatments on curved or irregular skin surfaces where maintaining laser focus is challenging. Optimized spacer selection can enhance treatment consistency and improve procedural accuracy, especially for small‐spot fractional lasers.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: This study was conducted in accordance with ethical standards and relevant institutional guidelines. Human tissue used in this study was obtained under a protocol classified as “Not Human Subjects Research” by the institutional review board (IRB) of Massachusetts General Hospital. As such, IRB approval and informed consent were not required. All tissue samples were fully deidentified before receipt and use in the study, and no patient‐identifiable information was accessed or utilized.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.
